extracted_codes.context: Structured problem list – The descendant traversal of SNOMEDCT code 84114007 (heart failure)

clinical_criteria: Ejection fraction < 50% (HF with reduced EF, HF Type = 1)
Ejection fraction ≥ 50% (HF with preserved EF, HF Type = 2)
Framingham Heart Failure Criteria: Diagnosis requires at least 2 major criteria OR 1 major + 2 minor criteria

Major criteria: Paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, neck vein distention, rales, radiographic cardiomegaly, acute pulmonary edema, S3 gallop, increased central venous pressure (>16 cm H2O at right atrium), hepatojugular reflux, weight loss >4.5 kg in 5 days in response to treatment

Minor criteria: Bilateral ankle edema, nocturnal cough, dyspnea on ordinary exertion, hepatomegaly, pleural effusion, decrease in vital capacity by one third from maximum recorded, tachycardia (heart rate >120 beats/min)
EF measurement timing: Use lowest EF measured 0-182 days after HF date, or 0-182 days prior, or 183-365 days after, or 183-365 days prior
ICD-9 and NLP dates within 365 days = definite HF
ICD-9 or NLP dates within 365-1825 days = probable HF
Either ICD-9 or NLP alone with EF <50% = possible HF
algorithm_summary: This phenotype algorithm identifies heart failure cases from EHR data using a combination of ICD-9-CM diagnosis codes (428.X), natural language processing of clinical notes to identify HF terms (multi-organ failure, cardiac failure, CHF, heart failure, ventricular failure), and ejection fraction measurements. Cases are classified into four categories: definite HF (requires both ICD-9 code and positive NLP mention within 365 days), probable HF (either ICD-9 or NLP with dates 365-1825 days apart), possible HF (either criterion alone if EF <50%), or control (no evidence). Heart failure is further subtyped based on ejection fraction: reduced EF (<50%) or preserved EF (≥50%). The algorithm uses Framingham Heart Failure Criteria for validation and incorporates structured data, medications (mapped to RxNorm), and echocardiography results through both structured data and NLP extraction.
